questions.question: Explain the characteristics of IoT data with emphasis on Volume, Velocity, and Variety.
keyPoints: Volume: IoT generates massive amounts of data - smart factories produce terabytes daily, smart cities generate petabytes/year.
Velocity: Data is generated at extremely high speeds requiring real-time processing - autonomous vehicles generate 1 GB/second.
Variety: Data comes in multiple formats - structured (sensor readings), semi-structured (JSON logs), and unstructured (video, audio).
Additional characteristics include Veracity (data quality), Variability (inconsistent flow rates), and Value (extracting meaningful insights).

questions.question: Describe the complete IoT Analytics Pipeline with all its stages. Explain the role of each stage and the technologies typically used.
keyPoints: Stage 1 - Data Generation: IoT devices and sensors generate raw data through measurements and event detection.
Stage 2 - Data Collection: Data gathered through IoT gateways, message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ), and protocol handlers (MQTT, CoAP).
Stage 3 - Data Storage: Stored in time-series databases, NoSQL databases (Cassandra), distributed file systems (HDFS), or cloud storage.
Stage 4 - Data Processing: Processed using batch frameworks (Hadoop MapReduce, Spark) or stream processing (Storm, Flink).
Stage 5 - Data Analysis: Analyzed using statistical analysis, machine learning, pattern recognition, and anomaly detection.
Stage 6 - Data Visualization: Insights presented through dashboards (Grafana, Kibana), BI tools (Tableau), or custom applications.
Stage 7 - Action and Feedback: Insights drive automated control commands, alerts, business decisions, and system optimizations.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the four types of analytics (Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive) with IoT examples.
keyPoints: Descriptive: Summarizes what happened - e.g., monthly average temperature readings from sensors, equipment utilization reports.
Diagnostic: Explains why it happened - e.g., root cause analysis of a machine failure by correlating vibration and temperature data.
Predictive: Forecasts what will happen - e.g., predicting equipment failure 2 weeks in advance using ML models on sensor data.
Prescriptive: Recommends what to do - e.g., automatically scheduling maintenance at optimal time and ordering replacement parts.

questions.question: Compare Edge Analytics and Cloud Analytics in IoT. Discuss their architecture, use cases, advantages, and limitations. When would you use each approach?
keyPoints: Edge Analytics: Processing at device/gateway level, provides low latency (milliseconds), reduces bandwidth costs, operates during connectivity loss, limited computational resources.

Cloud Analytics: Centralized processing with vast resources, supports complex ML models, handles historical analysis across all devices, higher latency (seconds to minutes), requires reliable connectivity.
Edge use cases: Real-time anomaly detection, safety-critical alerts, latency-sensitive control loops, bandwidth-constrained environments.
Cloud use cases: Historical trend analysis, cross-device correlation, model training, complex visualization dashboards, long-term storage.
Hybrid approach (most common): Edge handles time-critical decisions locally while sending data to cloud for deeper analysis, model training, and reporting.
